hook: This temple is a World Heritage site. In spite of the main temple having collapsed, it still remains an architectural masterpiece. Which temple is this?
explanation: The Sun Temple of Konark was built in the 13th century. It was conceived as a gigantic solar chariot with 12 pairs of exquisitely ornamented wheels dragged by seven horses. It originally consisted of a Shikhara and Jagmohana. The Shikara is now collapsed and only the Jagmohana is intact though with severe damage.

hook: This town has a unique Dusssehrah celebration where more than two hundred deities from the region congregate in the city centre for seven days. Which town is this?
explanation: The Kullu Dussehrah starts on Vijayadasami, the day when it ends in rest of India, and continues for a week after that. Each year about 225 Devtas arrive on foot to Kullu, carried by their own villagers. They are invited by the presiding deity of the region, Lord Raghunathji. The Rath Yatra of Lord Raghunathji kicks off the week long celebration. Each Devta has a designated area in the city centre and the villagers accompanying the Devta create a makeshift tent with kitchen and staying area. For a week, the entire city centre resembles a massive temple complex.

hook: The industrial group of Birlas built several temples in modern India. This is one among them. Where is this hillock temple?
explanation: The Lord Venkateshwara temple is one of Hyderabad’s holiest temples. The temple was built in 1976 on Neeladri hills of Hyderabad at an elevation of about 300 feet.. A towering, nine-and-a-half feet high idol of Lord Venketshwara in black granite is enshrined in the main Garbhagriha. The imposing 51 feet Rajagopuram, designed with South Indian influences, is an added attraction._x000D_ _x000D_ The temple projects the concept of universality and unity of religions and universal brotherhood among all religions through panels devoted to Guru Nanak Dev, Guru Gobind Singh, Lord Buddha, Bhagawan Mahaveera and Jesus Christ. People of all religion, race, caste and creed are free to visit the temple.

hook: Karnataka has a significant Jain population and has many Jain pilgrimage centres. This is one among them. Which city is this?
explanation: Karkala is near Mangalore. It attained political and cultural importance from the time of Kalasa-Karkala or Bhairarasa Odeyas (13th to 16th century AD). Jainism gained popularity during this period in centres such as Moodibadri and Karkala. The Chaturmukha Basti was built around 1586 AD. This is the most celebrated structural temple in Karkala and is referred to as Tribhuvana-tilaka-Jina-Caityalaya. The basti has four identical looking entrances from the four quarters leading to the garbhagriha and hence is popularly known as 'Chaturmukha Basti'. The Chaturmukha Basti is built in the form of a square mandapa. It has life size statues of three Tirthankaras besides small images of 24 Tirthankaras and Padmavati Yakshi in black stone.

hook: This hanuman temple at hilltop is at one of India's popular tourist locations. Which city is this?
explanation: The Jakhoo temple is at the top of the ridge and is the highest point in Shimla. It marks the place where Hanumanji heard Sage Yakoo’s chanting and stopped, before resuming his search for Sanjeevini. The temple complex has a 108 feet tall Hanumanji who looks down at the mall road and Shimla ridge below. The sunrise and the sunset are fabulous at Jakhoo. The slanting rays break through the tree leaves to light up the saffron coloured temple walls from different angles.

hook: This is one of the most picturesque locations of a Shakti temple and has written accounts of cannibalism. Where is this temple?
explanation: Sarahan marks the killing of King Banasur by Lord Krishna and the kingdom that Krishna’s son, Pradyumna, started. It is deemed to have had an uninterrupted reign till modern times. The Bhimkali temple is one of the 51 Shaktipeeths (as per local belief though it is not formally recognised as one) and Sati’s ear is supposed to have fallen here. The temple also has a fearsome reputation of human sacrifice until 17th century, which travellers have written about. The temple is mix of stone, wood and slate, it both stands out and blends into the landscape.

hook: This is a Shakti temple that is almost 1300 years old. It is in a remote location and not part of major travel routes. Where is this temple?
explanation: The Hateshwari temple is at Hatkoti between the Uttarkashi and the Shimla districts. There are five near identical stone temples that are called Pandavas’ toys. These are in the Nagara style. The temples of Hateshwari (Mahishasuramardini) and Shiva are in the pent roof style. The deity and inscriptions are dated around 1300 years old. The varying styles themselves indicate centuries of history around this temple complex.

hook: India has a number of step wells. Many of them are linked with a temple. This five storey step well is in the vicinity of a major town in India. Can you recognise the step well and guess where is located.
explanation: Adalaj step well is on the outskirts of Ahmedabad, about 20 kms from the city centre. The step well is about 600 years old. It was started by Queen Rudabai and completed by the conqueror of her kingdom Mahmud Begada.

hook: This cave complex is one of the most well known in India. It is also a World Heritage Site. Which complex is this?
explanation: Ajanta caves are near Aurangabad in Maharashtra. The caves, famous for their murals, are also great examples of sculptures. The caves were excavated in different periods (circa. 2nd century B.C. to 6th century A.D.) In all, total 30 excavations were hewn out of rock. The caves were both living quarters and worship areas.

hook: This Shiva temple is in the suburbs of a major city of India. Its architecture is unique and it has thousand years of history. Near which city is this temple?
explanation: The temple is in Ambernath, a suburb of Mumbai which is connected by the local train network. It was built by King Mahamandaleshwar of the Shilahar dynasty at the beginning of the last millennium. It is built in the style of Hemadpanthi temples which is a subset of the Vesara style. The temple has a mandapa with entrance on three sides. The Shivling is considered Swayambhu (naturally formed) and is set about 8 feet below.

hook: This temple for Rama is located on the banks of one of India's major rivers. Here Lord Rama appears not in human form but as Vaikuntha Rama with four hands. Can you guess where this temple is?
explanation: Badrachalam is on the banks of the river Godavari. It is considered as one the places where Lord Rama lived. It is also believed that Sita was abducted by Ravana from Badrachalam, a belief which is also attributed to Nashik. Unlike in other places, Lord Rama appears with four hands, carrying traditional bow and arrow; and also Sankha and Chakra, characteristic of Lord Vishnu.

hook: The Char Dhams of Himalayas are a major pilgrimage route. Which of the Char Dhams of Himalayas is this temple?
explanation: The original Char Dham Yatra covers Badrinath, Puri, Rameswaram and Dwarka. The Char Dhams of Himalayas are called Chota Char Dhams. Dham means the abode of God. Badrinath is part of the original Char Dham as well as the Chota Char Dham of Himalayas.

hook: This glacier is the source of one of the grandest rivers of India. Which river is this?
explanation: Gaumukh is the mouth of river Baghirathi. It is about 18 kms away from Gangotri and requires a long walk on glacial moraine. At Gaumukh, Baghirathi emerges from the glacier as a river. Gaumukh is a sacred pilgrimage.

hook: This temple is a successor in architecture to another well known temple. Both these temples are part of World Heritage sites of India. Which temple is this?
explanation: The temple at Gangaikonda Cholapuram is a short distance away from the Big Temple at Tanjore. It is built by Rajendra Chola, the son of Raja Raja who built the Big Temple. Is commemorates the conquest of the gangetic region by Rajendra Chola. Its architecture is similar to that of the Big Temple and it slightly shorter than the Big Termple, in deference to Raja Raja. Both these temples, along with Darasuram temple, are part of the Living Chola Temples in the UNESO World Heritage list.

hook: Which well known Sikh pilgrimage site is this?
explanation: The Harmandir Saheb or Golden Temple is the holiest of the Sikh temples and is in Amritsar.

hook: These rock sculptures mark a sacred site for Jains. In which city are you likely to find these?
explanation: The Gwalior Fort is atop a cliff. The Jain community had carved immense sculptures on the vertical rock faces of the hill. These are spread over five locations around the fort. These sculptures date to 15th century AD though some of them are believed to be from the 7th Century AD. These are immense in size but were destroyed by the army of Babur. Jains continue their worship in some of the sites.

hook: This temple marks the spot where Lord Shiva hid from Pandavas as they sought him out after the Kurukshetra war. Which location is this?
explanation: Guptkashi is considered as a very sacred site. After the Kurukshetra war, Pandavas wanted to atone for the sins committed during the war. They sought Shiva who was not willing to meet them and disappeared from Kashi. Pandavas pursued him to Guptkashi where he was hiding as a Nandi. When Pandavas recognised the Nandi it disappeared and went into the ground. Guptkashi is set high above the banks of the Mandakini river on the way to Kedarnath.

hook: This is one of the famous temples of Lord Krishna in India. Where is this temple?
explanation: The deity in Guruvayoor is Lord Mahavishnu, in the form of Krishnavatara. (No Suggestions) is called as the Dwarka of the south, as the original image is believed to be from Dwarka. When Dwarka was submerged, Guru and Vayu were tasked by Krishna to install the deity in a spot equally holy and eventually located Guruvayoor. It is extremely popular for Annaprasana, where infants are fed their first solid meal. The temple has elaborate rituals throughout the day and is one of the most visited temples of South India.

hook: This Nandi is one among the most beautifully carved Nandis in India. The temple that houses the Nandi is famous for exquisite sandstone sculptures. Which temple is this?
explanation: The Hoysala era is one that contributed enormously to the development of several creative fields as well as spiritual and humanistic thought. During their reign, the Hoysalas built more than 1500 temples all across their empire of which only a little over 100 survive today. The Hoysaleshwara temple at Halebidu is the most exemplary architectural ensemble of the Hoysalas extant today. Built in 1121CE during the reign of the Hoysala King, Vishnuvardhana Hoysaleshwara. The temple is dedicated to Shiva. More sculpturally and artistically sophisticated than any other Hoysala temple, the Hoysaleshwara temple is most well-known for the more than 240 wall sculptures that run all along the outer wall. Invaders from North India finally succeeded in pillaging the city in 1310.

hook: Along which river is this gathering taking place?
explanation: This is the Ganga Aarti at Har Ki Pauri. Haridwar is one of the holiest pilgrimage destinations in India. It is considered as the gateway to the land of Vishnu as well as Shiva. The Ganga levels out into plains at Haridwar and thus it is also called Gangadwara. Haridwar is one of the locations of Kumbh and is also considered one among the seven locations that give Moksha.

hook: This circular temples houses 64 deities of Yoginis along its circle inner wall. This is a rare architectural form of an Indian temple. Though heavily defaced, the deities are fine examples of stone sculpture. Near which city are you likely to find this Chausat Yogini temple?
explanation: The Yogini temples are form of temples believed to belong to the Tantric sect. These are rare temples in India. The Hirapur temple was discovered only in 1953 though it is very near Bhubaneswar. Generally in circular form, the Yogini temples are called Chausat Yogini temples due to 64 Yoginis installed in some of them, though others have varying numbers such as 81 or 42. The Hirapur temple sculptures are exquisite in spite of having been damaged. It is on the outskirts of Bhubaneswar.

hook: In this temple in Tamil Nadu, Krishna is worshipped in his cowherd form. The deity poses with a cow. Which city is this?
explanation: The Rajagopala Swamy temple in Mannargudi is about 1000 years old and went through major expansion during the Nayak period in 17th century. The temple takes its name from the deity Rajagopala who is depicted holding a whip in the right hand and leaning on a cow with the left hand. The deity is considered as one of the most beautiful bronze sculptures of India. The temple has a 154 feet high, 11 tiered Gopuram and has seven enclosures. The temple tank covers an area of 25 acres and is considered one of the largest in India.

hook: In this temple, Shiva takes the form of a physician and cures devotees of ill health. It is one among the most beautiful hill temples of Shiva. Which temple is this?
explanation: Baijnath in Himachal Pradesh is set against the backdrop of Dauladhar mountains. It is a stone temple built in 1204 at a site where worship was already being practiced. It is built in the Nagara style of temple architecture. It has wonderful carvings on the periphery and two delightful balconies on either side of the main temple. It is close to Dharamshala, the paragliding hotspot of Bir Biling and the tea gardens of Palampur.

hook: This group of miniature temples were in decrepit state. These were painstakingly rehabilitated by ASI. About half of the original temples have been restored. Where is this complex?
explanation: The Bateshwar temples are a cluster of almost 200 mini grandeurs made of sandstone, devoted to lords Shiva and Vishnu. Spread over 25 acres, these shrines were built across slanted hills near Padavali. The temples were constructed in the 8th to 10th century AD probably during the Gurjara-Pratihara Dynasty. The complex was dilapidated. About 60 temples were restored by Padmashri K.K Muhammed of ASI beginning in the year 2005.

hook: This temple has a characteristic architectural style and is considered to have some of the finest soapstone temple sculptures in India. Which temple is this?
explanation: The Hoysala era is one that contributed enormously to the development of several creative fields as well as spiritual and humanistic thought. During their reign, the Hoysalas built more than 1500 temples all across their empire of which only a little over 100 survive today. Belur was the first capital city of the Hoysalas. The Chennakeshava temple complex was at the centre of the old walled town located on the banks of the Yagachi River. Construction of the temple commenced in 1117 AD and took a 103 years to complete. A total of 118 stone inscriptions have been recovered from the temple complex. The Chennakeshava temple was devoted to Vishnu. The richly sculptured exterior of the temple includes sculptures and iconography and horizontal friezes that depict scenes from daily life, music, and dance, and narrate scenes from the life of Vishnu and his reincarnations and the epics, Ramayana, and Mahabharata. However, some of the representations of Shiva are also included. Consecrated on a sacred site, the temple has remained continuously worshipped since its establishment and remains until today as a site of pilgrimage for Vaishnavites.

hook: This tank is historically linked to Ramayana. It can be found amidst the city centre in a major Indian city; and is in stark contrast to its surroundings. Which city is this?
explanation: Lord Rama is supposed to have shot an arrow into the ground to make Ganga spring forth. Banganga was also the location of the hermitage of Sage Gautam. He advised Lord Rama to worship Lord Shiva to get back his wife. Accordingly Lord Rama made a shivling from the sand (Valuka in Sanskrit) and so the word Walkeshwar Mahadev of the Walkeshwar temple.

hook: This temple has a characteristic architectural style and is considered to have some of the finest soapstone temple sculptures in India. Which temple is this?
explanation: The Hoysala era is one that contributed enormously to the development of several creative fields as well as spiritual and humanistic thought. During their reign, the Hoysalas built more than 1500 temples all across their empire of which only a little over 100 survive today. Belur was the first capital city of the Hoysalas. The Chennakeshava temple complex was at the centre of the old walled town located on the banks of the Yagachi River. Construction of the temple commenced in 1117 AD and took a 103 years to complete. A total of 118 stone inscriptions have been recovered from the temple complex. The Chennakeshava temple was devoted to Vishnu. The richly sculptured exterior of the temple includes sculptures and iconography and horizontal friezes that depict scenes from daily life, music, and dance, and narrate scenes from the life of Vishnu and his reincarnations and the epics, Ramayana, and Mahabharata. However, some of the representations of Shiva are also included. Consecrated on a sacred site, the temple has remained continuously worshipped since its establishment and remains until today as a site of pilgrimage for Vaishnavites.

hook: This temple complex is in a remote location in India. It houses 84 deities in an open air temple complex. It is considered more than 1200 years old. Which place is this?
explanation: Bharmour is surrounded by 16,000 feet high mountains on three sides. It is set deep within Chamba district of Himachal Pradesh and is one of the remotest locations of India. The Chaurasi temple complex at Bharmour is at a remarkable setting, open on five sides. It is a collection of temples, 84, believed to represent Lord Shiva’s 84 companions on his journey to Manimahesh. Many temples are tiny and many deities share a common space. The central temple of Manimahesh stands alongside a deodhar tree, almost double its height. The present temple structure is atleast 1100 years old.

hook: This temple is one of the most well known temples of India. It is also a World Heritage Site where traditional worship is still practiced. Which temple is this?
explanation: The Big Temple or the Brahadeeswara Temple is the grandest architectural achievement of the Chola dynasty of Tamil Nadu. The temple was consecrated in the year 1010 by Rajaraja I, the most prominent of the Chola kings. It is built entirely of granite and the Vimana of the temple consists of 13 storeys and is 66 mts tall. The Linga continues to be under worship and is 4 mts tall.

hook: This temple is at a hilltop near a tourist destination. It has a very interesting and unique legend. Which temple is this?
explanation: The Bijli Mahadev temple is situated at the edge of a cliff overlooking the confluence of the rivers Beas and Parvati. It is regularly struck by lightning breaking the Shiv Ling, which is then put together by application of ghee. The name Bijli Mahadev is a result of these events. A tall staff with a metallic tip serves as a lightning attraction. There are signs warning the visitors not to touch the staff. It is a short trek from the road head approachable from Kullu.

hook: Nandi is one of the major architectural features of Shaivite temples. This Nandi in granite is considered as one of the largest monolith Nandis of India. Near which temple is this Nandi?
explanation: The Nandi at Chamundeswari is about 16 feet in height and 24 feet long and is the third largest in India. It is not a part of the temple complex but is in its vicinity. The Nandi is more than 350 years old and was built by the then Maharaja of Mysore.Originally white in colour, repeated offering of oil and its oxidation resulted in the Nandi looking pitch black. The Nandi was cleaned by the ASI and was restored to its original white colour recently.

hook: Shiva is worshipped in this temple in his dancing form. Also, this temple is one among the five temples in this region dedicated to each element of the universe, this being dedicated to Space. Which temple is this?
explanation: The Chidambaram Nataraja temple in Tamil Nadu is about thousand years old and has been continuously restored, expanded and renovated. The deity is a rare representation of Shiva in his dancing form, and not as a traditional Linga. The temple is also renowned for its representation of the 108 poses of Bharatanatyam in sculpture and for the Malabar style Vimana. The temple is also one of the five Shiva temples in south India which represent the five elements of the Universe. Chidambaram represents space. The other four temples of the group represent water, fire, air and earth.

hook: This temple is called Curfew Wali Mata mandir since prior to the construction of the temple, the site was in dispute and clashes used to occur frequently. Local curfew was a regular feature. In which Indian city is this temple?
explanation: The temple site was a location for Navratri celebration and devotees used to worship Durga on a temporary platform. When a decision was taken to build a permanent temple, disputes arose and the local Government imposed curfew in the area. The movement to build a temple continued and eventually the temple was built and consecrated amidst curfew. The locals named the temple Curfew Wali Mata Mandir and the name continues till date. The temple, though small, is very popular and there is a steady stream of worshippers at all times.

hook: This temple is grand in sculpture. With two other more popular temples, this temple forms a trio of World Heritage sites in India. Where is this temple located?
explanation: Darasuram is a small settlement on the outskirts of Kumbakonam. It is just 34 kms from the Big Temple at Tanjore. The Airavateshvara temple is considered one of the finest monument of the Chola era. Though not of the same size as the Big Temple, the Darasuram temple is famous for its fine sculptures. The temple was conceived in the form of a chariot. Prancing horses pulling wheels are carved on the basement.

hook: This is one of the five Prayags or confluences that are located along the course of Ganga in Uttarakhand. This is where Ganga forms first. Which is this Prayag?
explanation: Devprayag is the first Prayag from Rishikesh on the way to Badrinath. At Devprayag, Alaknanda and Baghirathi merge to form Ganga. A temple of Raghunathji is at the confluence and is believed to be under worship for 2000 years.

hook: This is one of the five Prayags or confluences that are located along the course of Ganga in Uttarakhand. Which is this Prayag?
explanation: Karnaprayag is where Karna meditated and worshipped the Sun God, his father. He is also believed to be cremated here. Karnaprayag connects the two distinct regions of Uttarakhand - Garhwal and Kumaon. Here the Pindar river joins Alaknanda.

hook: This is one of the five Prayags or confluences that are located along the course of Ganga in Uttarakhand. Which is this Prayag?
explanation: Vishnuprayag is the last Prayag before Badrinath. Dhauliganga joins Alaknanda at this secluded confluence. There is no permanent settlement. Vishnuprayag is believed to mark the place where Narada prayed to Vishnu and was released from his human form.

hook: This is one of the five Prayags or confluences that are located along the course of Ganga in Uttarakhand. Which is this Prayag?
explanation: Nandaprayag marks the confluence of Nandakini with Alaknanda. It is the fourth prayag on the way to Badrinath from Rishikesh. Unlike other Prayags, the two rivers flow gentle and wide, the confluence is broader.

hook: This monastery complex is one of the oldest in India. It is located on a cliff and overlooks a spectacular river confluence in Spiti region in Himachal Pradesh. Which is this monastery?
explanation: The Dhankar monastery is on a mud cliff, all sides and edges ragged by raging winds. Dhankar has a monastery and a fort. They overlook the spectacular and broad confluence of two great rivers, Pin and Spiti, which eventually become the Sutlej. The Dhankar fort has at least three separate structures. In each structure there are chambers one above the other. Several windows look out into the Spiti valley and the confluence of the rivers.

hook: This stupa is new. However the location marks one of the oldest historical sites of India. Which location is this?
explanation: The first Walkeshwar Temple was constructed in 1127 AD by a GSB minister, Lakshman Prabhu, in the court of Shilahar Kings in Thane. It was reconstructed by the then famous Rama Kamati in 1715 AD.

hook: The Char Dhams of Himalayas are a major pilgrimage route. Which of the Char Dhams of Himalayas is this temple?
explanation: Originally Char Dham Yatra referred to Badrinath, Puri, Dwarka and Rameswaram. The Char Dham Yatra of Himalayas is referred to as the Chota Char Dham Yatra. It covers Gangotri and Yamunotri, the farthest points of access to the rivers, Kedarnath and Badrinath. Gangotri and Badrinath have a motorable road, while Yamunotri and Kedarnath require a trek.

hook: This festival is one of the most vivid and public festivals of India. In which are you likely to find this sight?
explanation: The Ganpati festival is one of the most public celebrations in India. Ganpati is welcomed to households; and to neighbourhoods. Some deities are small and made of clay while giant Ganpatis are made out of plaster of paris. The deities are worshipped for several days starting Vinayak Chaturthi and then immersed in lakes or in the Sea. The last day of immersion is ten days after Vinayak Chaturthi.

hook: Nataraja is the famous dancing form of Shiva. Which of these towns has a famous Nataraja temple?
explanation: _x000D_ _x000D_ Nataraja is the most aesthetic form of Shiva, blending force and grace. According to Hindu belief, all life is a part of the great rhythmic process of creation and destruction. Shiva''s dance symbolizes the eternal life-death rhythm going on in an endless cycle._x000D_ _x000D_ Shiva is the presiding deity of Bharatanatya, the art and science of dancing. He has danced all the hundred and eight kinds of dances the Bharatanatya talks about._x000D_ _x000D_ The most famous temple for Nataraja is in Chidambaram. Beautiful deities of Nataraja are also seen near Kancheepuram. Several temples in Tamil Nadu have a shrine dedicated to Nataraja._x000D_ _x000D_ This bronze sculpture is in Chennai Egmore museum. It is set against an artificial backdrop of the cosmos._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: SR Ramanujam

hook: As Kalasamharamurti, Shiva stopped Yama from claiming his devotee's life. Whose life was it?
explanation: _x000D_ _x000D_ _x000D_ _x000D_ Sage Mrikandu and his wife Maruthavathi were blessed with a Son by Shiva. But there was a condition. The son would be bad and live long (or) he would be brilliant with only 16 years of life. They preferred a brilliant Son and Shiva fulfilled their desire. Markandeya was born to them._x000D_ _x000D_ Markandeya grew up as a wise Son and became a votary of Shiva. As he reached 16 years of age, he wished to worship Shiva in a temple. His parents agreed. As Markandeya was praying, Yama threw his noose around his neck. Markandeya hugged the Shiva Linga but Yama would not let go. He claimed Markandeya's life as he had already lived the 16 years he was destined for. At this instant, Shiva emerged from the Linga as Kalasamharamurti. Shiva knocked down Yama. The life of Markandeya was saved._x000D_ _x000D_ Tirukadaiyur in Tamil Nadu has the famous deity of Kalasamharamurti. Markandeya is believed to have visited Tirukadaiyur. The fiery third eye is well marked in the deity here._x000D_ _x000D_ This is Kalasamharamurti in Nanjangud, Karnataka._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Ugra Twitter

hook: Bhairava emerged from Shiva to slay Andhaka. From where did Bhairava emerge?
explanation: _x000D_ Bhairava is endowed with projecting tusks and protruding eyes. He commands awe and veneration instilling a sense of fear. He is adored as the guardian deity of settlements. He carries a Damaru, an Ankusa and a skull. The dog accompanies him as Vahana. He is glorified as Kshetrapala (Protector of Earth). He sports matted lock of scarlet color and is adorned with garlands of snakes and skulls._x000D_ _x000D_ Andhaka was the son of Hiranyaksha who was a votary of Shiva. With his invincible might and power granted by Shiva, he started oppressing and harassing the celestials. Unable to bear the unending atrocities, the celestials prayed to Shiva for help. Provoked by the insolence of Andhaka, Shiva opened his third eye. From the flames of the third eye Bhairava emerged with projecting tusks and protruding eyes accompanied by a horrifying hunting sound._x000D_ _x000D_ Bhairava and Andhaka engaged in a fierce battle. Bhairava pounced on Andhaka and plunged his trident into his head. At the time of death, Andhaka realized his heinous acts and repented them. Lord Shiva revived his life and also took him up as the chief of his retinue._x000D_ _x000D_ There are eight forms of Bhairava in the city of Kashi itself. Bhairava is very popular in Karnataka and Andhra Pradesh also._x000D_ _x000D_ This is Bhairava guarding Sringeri in Karnataka._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Sringeri Mutt

hook: The legend of Veerabhadra resulted in a group of sacred tirthas in India. Which group is it?
explanation: _x000D_ _x000D_ Daksha was the Son of Brahma. His daughter Sati was married to Shiva, Rudra. Daksha was performing a great yagna lasting many many years. Daksha felt offended as Rudra did not pay respects to him during the yagna. Since then Rudra and Daksha were not in good terms. Daksha continued the Yagna._x000D_ _x000D_ As the Yagna was nearing culmination, several Devas were headed there. Sati heard about this and wished to go to her father's house. She told Rudra that the rightful place for a daughter then is to be at her father's place. Rudra advised her against going citing that Daksha would not respect her. Sati did not pay heed and went to the Yagna without Rudra._x000D_ _x000D_ As predicted by Rudra, Sati was humiliated at the Yagna. Unable to bear this, she gave up her life in the Yagna. Rudra became ferocious on hearing this news. He took the form of Veerabhadra. His head touched the skies. His three eyes resembled three suns. He wore a garland of skulls. He reduced the place of Yagna to ashes. He then beheaded Daksha._x000D_ _x000D_ He picked up the body of Sati and started Tandava, the dance of destruction. Frightened by the Tandava the Devas pleaded to Vishnu to stop the destruction. Vishnu cut off the body of Sati into several parts with his Sudarshana Chakra so that Rudra would eventually stop the Tandava. The body parts of Sati fell into 51 sacred tirthas all over India which are called Shakti Peeths._x000D_ _x000D_ The site of Yagna is in Kankhal close to Haridwar in Uttarakhand. The picture is of the Daksheshwara temple in Kankhal._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: SR Ramanujam

hook: Who does Shiva defeat in his form as Gajasamharamurti?
explanation: _x000D_ _x000D_ The sages of Dharukavana were conceited. They thought Karma is everything and there is no need for God. They were also infuriated with Shiva as he distracted their wives. They performed a sacrifice known as Abhichara Yagna. They raised a huge and disastrous fire swearing vengeance on Shiva. Several ghosts, demons, snakes and monstrous animals came forth from the fire. A mighty and monstrous elephant emerged from the fire followed by snakes, a terrifying tiger and wild antelope. This was followed by a demon._x000D_ _x000D_ As a Yogi, Shiva was calm and collected. As the elephant charged at him, he assumed a miniature form and entered the body of the elephant. Shiva then tore open the body of the elephant and reappeared. He stood on the head of the animal and held its skin in his hands. He adorned other creatures as ornaments and snakes as garlands. He subdued the demons. The form of Shiva as Gajasamharamurti depicts his tearing the monstrous elephant and wearing its hide as his attire._x000D_ _x000D_ Sankaracharya admires Gajasamharamurti in his Sivananda Lahari. He compares the human mind with a wild elephant running amok. It has to be tamed like a mahout does. This great truth is symbolically shown in the form of Gajasamharamurti._x000D_ _x000D_ This sculpture is in Belur, Karnataka_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: BY SA Wikimedia Commons

hook: Shiva absorbs the fury of Ganga as she flows down in deference to his devotee's wishes. Who was the devotee?
explanation: _x000D_ _x000D_ Gangadhara is associated with the perseverance of Bhagiratha. Siva is depicted bearing and sustaining the furious torrents of the river Ganga, as she descends from heaven to grace Bhagiratha._x000D_ _x000D_ Bhagiratha's sixty thousand ancestors had wrongly accused that Sage Kapila had stolen their father's horse. Therefore they were burnt to ashes by Sage Kapila. The ancestors would attain redemption only when Gangaji washed their ashes. Bhagiratha did penance to bring Ganga to earth._x000D_ _x000D_ Even though Bhagiratha managed to convince Ganga to come to earth, someone had to stop the fury of Ganga as she descends from heaven. So Bhagiratha prayed to Shiva and convinced him to absorb the fury of Gangaji. As Gangadhara, Shiva stood astride with his feet firmly on the ground. He untied his plait of hair and loosened it so as to bear the mighty torrents of Ganga._x000D_ _x000D_ Bhagiratha's troubles were not over yet. Gangaji had inundated the hermitage of Sage Janhu who then swallowed all the flow of Ganga. Bhagiratha then begged forgiveness from Sage Janhu who then let out Gangaji through his ears. She would eventually reach the ashram of Sage Kapila and wash the ashes of Bhagiratha's ancestors._x000D_ _x000D_ Bhagiratha thus stands as a symbol of perseverance. He is endowed with courage, confidence, determination, endurance, tenacity of purpose, holy mission and a holy vision. He is a hero bearing an exemplary character to be emulated by everyone on earth._x000D_ _x000D_ This is in Elephanta island off Mumbai._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Rangan Datta

hook: Sage Bringi refused to worship Shakti and this gave rise to a well known form of Shiva. What is this form?
explanation: _x000D_ _x000D_ Ardhanariswara represents the blending of force and grace that contributes to the creation of life. They are complementary and not contradictory._x000D_ _x000D_ The beetle is connected to the origin of Ardhanariswara. Once a sage was very devoted to worshipping Shiva. He was so devoted that he refused to worship Shakti. Shiva wanted to correct his views and show that both him and Shakti are one only. He made Shakti sit very close to him so that the sage has to worship both. But the sage outwitted Shiva by taking the form of a beetle (Bringa) and entering through the small gap between Shiva and Shakti._x000D_ _x000D_ Shiva then shared his body with Shakti. The sage now realized that Shiva and Shakti are one only. He begged forgiveness. Shiva and Shakti welcomed him to Kailasa. Since the sage took the form of Bringa (beetle) he came to be called Bringi._x000D_ _x000D_ This beautiful bronze deity is in Chennai Egmore museum._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: SR Ramanujam

hook: Somaskanda is a form of Shiva with an attendant deity. Who is this deity?
explanation: _x000D_ _x000D_ Shiva, Parvati and Skanda (Subramanya) are worshipped as Somaskanda. Skanda stands between the divine mother and divine father._x000D_ _x000D_ Surapadma was once creating terror in Devaloka. The devas approached Shiva and sought help. Shiva agreed to help them. He then fell into a trance. During the trance his third eye opened and six sparks sprang forth. They were carried by Vayu to Saravana Poigai (divine waters). They were transformed into babies. Uma and Shiva came to Saravana Poigai. Uma clasped the babies to her bosom. All the babies became united by her divine touch. Uma placed the baby, Skanda between her and Shiva. Skanda would kill the entire group of Surapadma._x000D_ _x000D_ This beautiful Somaskanda deity is in Salar Jung museum in Hyderabad._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Saiiko Wikimedia Commons

hook: Dakshinamurti is a very pleasing manifestation of Shiva. What does Shiva represent in this form?
explanation: _x000D_ _x000D_ Once when Vishnu was sleeping, four sages demanded entry into Vaikunta. The dwara palakas refused entry. The upset sages cursed the dwara palakas. They were to take birth a hundred times in earth and only then come back to Vaikunta._x000D_ _x000D_ Vishnu woke up and forgave the dwarapalakas and declared that they had to be reborn only thrice. Vishnu was also upset that the learned sages could not control their emotion and ego. He said, "You have betrayed yourselves by losing your temper. You will hasten to earth and take refuge at the feet of Dakshinamurti, the mighty conqueror of senses and the very symbol of knowledge and wisdom."_x000D_ _x000D_ The sages reached the hermitage of Dakshinamurti on earth. He guided them into the knowledge of self. Control of senses is an incidental learning in the knowledge of self._x000D_ _x000D_ The deity of Dakshinamurti is enshrined in Shiva temples in the prahara facing south. Dakshinamurti is represented as a handsome youth seated beneath a banyan tree and preaching to the aged sages. His right leg is dangling down vertically and is placed on the demon Apasmara. His left leg is bent across the right thigh. He has four hands. The rear ones hold a snake and a trident. The right fore arm is in the form of Chinmudra. It represents Jiva and Brahman. The left forehand holds palm leaves._x000D_ _x000D_ Dakshinamurti has a pleasing personality. He exudes calm and peace. The Great Teacher enlightens the sages by Divine Silence. Silence is more eloquent than speech._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Srinivasan GS Flickr

hook: Chandrasekhara is the manifestation of Shiva with the crescent moon on his head. What was the reason for Shiva to do this?
explanation: _x000D_ _x000D_ Shiva adorned Chandra to console him and to absolve him of a curse._x000D_ _x000D_ Chandra had married twenty seven daughters of Daksha, the son of Brahma. But he was partial to two of them. The rest of the daughters complained to Daksha. An infuriated Daksha cursed that Chandra would lose his brightness. Chandra repented his actions but Daksha would not forgive him. Chandra surrendered to Shiva who then adorned him in his head. Shiva also ordained that instead of losing the brightness forever, Chandra would wax and wane._x000D_ _x000D_ This beautiful bronze deity is unfortunately in Brooklyn museum._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Brooklyn Museum

hook: In this city Hanumanji lives as a protector of Lord Rama. He has his own temple. Which city is this?
explanation: Hanumanji resides in the Hanuman Garhi temple of Ayodhya as a protector of Ayodhya. Lord Ram himself is supposed to have given this responsibility when he left earth._x000D_ _x000D_ Befitting this role, the Hanumanji deity, though small in size, is atop a throne in Hanuman Garhi. The temple is a fortified structure with bastions in each corner. Like a fort, the temple is reached by climbing a short flight of steps. It is quite close to the Ram Janambhoomi site and is on the hilly contours of Ramkot. Pilgrims first visit Hanuman Garhi after a bath at Sarayu and before they visit Ram Janambhoomi._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ Ayodhya, UP Tourism_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ _x000D_ Ayodhya, UP Tourism

hook: Ashokan edicts and pillars are spread across the geography of India. One of them is at a famous site in Rama's journey. Which site is this?
explanation: Prayagraj houses an Ashokan pillar edict. The pillar marks an enormous continuity in the heritage of India._x000D_ _x000D_ Prayagraj is of great significance in Ramayana. It was chosen by Ashoka as the site of one of his pillars. Samudragupta marked his inscription on the same pillar. Later on Jehangir also added to it. All of them recognised its historical value and legacy._x000D_ _x000D_ Nashik does not have any Ashokan edict. Kalsi and Dhauli have rock edicts but these are not connected to Lord Rama's journey._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni.

hook: One of the most ancient geological features of India is connected to the journey of Lord Rama. Which among these features is it?
explanation: _x000D_ _x000D_ _x000D_ The Lonar lake and crater were created when a meteorite crashed into earth 5.70,000 years ago. Lord Rama is believed to have visited Lonar during his journey through Dhandakaranya forest. He performed the Shraddha of Dasharath here. Later, Shatrughan kills Lavanasura and sets up his capital here. The name Lonar is a modification of the name Lavanar._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni.

hook: Dr Ambedkar started his fight for temple entry for scheduled castes by entering this temple. Which temple is this?
explanation: Kala Ram temple in Nashik is a famous temple which was constructed in the 18th century by the Peshwas. It is so called because of the pitch black stone from which the deities are made. The temple probably existed from earlier times. It was reclaimed from invaders and the present grand structure was constructed by the Peshwas. There are puranic references that indicate a temple existed in this spot about 800 years ago._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ Ramanujam

hook: In which city would you find the pond Banganga, a pond which is supposed to have been created by Rama by shooting an arrow into the earth?
explanation: _x000D_ Banganga is a tank in the southern Mumbai near Malabar Hill. Rama and Lakshmana had come here in search of Ravana. They wanted to drink water and therefore Rama shot an arrow into the ground. The tank and the surrounding area now host a number of small temples. The tank preserves the look of yesteryears and is a sharp contrast to the surrounding high rises of Mumbai city._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ Ramanujam

hook: Kardiguda is a village near Hubli in Karnataka. It has a cave that has been closed up by a concrete structure because villagers fear that whoever ventured into it died. How is this connected to Ramayana?
explanation: _x000D_ Ayomukhi is an ogress who coveted Lakshmana as she saw him wandering with Rama. She transformed herself into a beautiful woman and implored Lakshmana to stay with her. When Lakshmana refused, she turned into her true form and grabbed him. Lakshmana cut off her nose, ears and breast. The ogress ran into her cave seeking safety. Nowadays, villagers have blocked off the cave with two foot thick concrete because ten villagers who ventured into the cave are believed to have died. There is a temple nearby in which only a female priest officiates since Ayomukhi does not kill women._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ Twitter @Yuva Brigade

hook: There are five holy lakes in Hinduism. One of them is linked to Ramayana. Which one is it?
explanation: Parvati prayed and bathed in Pampa Sarovar (in Karnataka) to get Lord Shiva as her husband._x000D_ _x000D_ When Sabri met Rama and Lakshmana, she led them to Pampa Sarovar. She indicated that they would meet Sugreeva there and that he will help them in the search for Ravana._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ www.facebook.compampasarovaranegundi

hook: The Kodanda Rama temple in Hampi has an unusual sculpture of someone praying to Rama, Lakshmana and Sita. Who is this?
explanation: A sculpture of Sugreeva is shown bowing to Rama, Lakshmana, Sita and Hanumaji in this temple. Sugreeva is supposed to have built this temple in gratitude to Rama. He then also installed his own sculpture marking his paying of respect. Source Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni. Picture Credit

hook: Dasaratha conducted the putrakameshti Yaga to beget children. A very unlikely location is believed to be the location where he conducted the Yaga. Which location is this?
explanation: The Adi Jagannatha temple in Tiruppullani near Rameswaram is believed to be the place where Dasaratha conducted the putrakameshti yaga. Even today, payasam is distributed as prasada in the morning and childless couples visit the temple to seek blessings._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ sadagopan.org

hook: Rambodha is a temple in Sri Lanka connected with the Ramayana Itihaasa. Which event is it connected to?
explanation: Rambodha hill marks the spot where Hanuman ji first landed in Sri Lanka. It is also the place where Rama's troops or "bodhas" camped. A hill to the southern side of the Rambodha hill is called Ravanbodha and is believed to be where Ravana's army camped. The Chinmaya Mission maintains a temple at Rambodha which has a twenty foot granite deity of Hanumanji._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ www.facebook.comweerasinghe.ruwan

hook: Which place is considered as the birthplace of Ravana?
explanation: _x000D_ _x000D_ Bisrakh in Noida, just a few kilometers outside Delhi is considered the birthplace of Ravana in India. Villagers of Bisrakh celebrate both Dussehrah and Diwali in a muted manner._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ _x000D_ UP Tourism

hook: Dunuwila is a location in Sri Lanka connected with the Ramayana itihaasa. What is the event that it is associated with?
explanation: Rama's battle with Ravana raged on. As Ravana perpetually thought of Sita in his heart, it is believed that Rama could not shoot an arrow at his heart. It was predicted that Ravana will get frustrated and think of revenge on Rama and forget Sita. At that time Rama would be able to kill Ravana as Sita was no longer in his heart. At Dunuwila, Rama is believed to have drawn the brahmastra that killed Ravana. Source Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni. Picture Credit

hook: The Muneeswaran temple in Colombo has a special place in Ramayana. Which event is associated with this place?
explanation: Muneeswara temple is supposed to have been one of the ancient Shiva temples. The name Mun Eswaran in Tamil implies early Shiva or before Shiva. This temple is believed to have been consecrated by Ravana himself or as the place where Ravana prayed to Shiva. As Rama had killed Ravana, a brahmin and a Shiv Bhakt, he is supposed to have come here to absolve himself of the sin. He was turned away and then created a small Shiva Lingam at a nearby spot called Manavari. Muneeswaran temple is one of the largest temples in Sri Lanka._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ munneswaram.com

hook: Nandigram is famous for the land acquisition dispute that triggered the decline of Left Front in West Bengal. There is another Nandigram just outside Ayodhya. What is it famous for?
explanation: _x000D_ _x000D_ Bharat ruled from Nandigram during Rama's exile. When Rama was returning to Ayodhya, he did not want to claim the kingdom against Bharat's wish. Therefore he sent Hanuman to assess the intent of Bharat. Hanuman came in disguise as a Brahmin and tested Bharat's will to welcome Rama._x000D_ _x000D_ The temple here shows Bharat and Hanuman in embrace after Hanuman revealed to Bharat his true form. The Jata Kund here is believed to be the place where Lord Rama and Lakshman cut their matted hair at the end of their exile._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ Uttar Pradesh A to Z, UP Tourism_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ _x000D_ UP Tourism

hook: When it was time to leave the earth, Lord Rama walked into Sarayu and disappeared. Which location on Sarayu/ Ayodhya did he disappear from?
explanation: Guptar Ghat is the location on the Sarayu river from where Rama left this world. It is called Gupt to represent the vanishing of Rama._x000D_ _x000D_ Source_x000D_ Footsteps of Rama, Vikrant Pande and Neelesh Kulkarni._x000D_ UP A to Z, UP Tourism_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit_x000D_ Ayodhya, UP Tourism

hook: This site of repeated attacks is the first Jyotirlinga listed in the Shiva Purana. Which is it?
explanation: Quick read: Soma (moon) got the curse of his father in law mitigated by praying to Shiva at Somnath. The authoritative list of Jyotirlingas is in the Shiva Purana. It is inscribed at Mahakaal temple, Ujjain. Somnath on the Saurashtra coast of Gujarat is the first on the list. Soma (moon) was married to the 27 daughters of Daksha (nakshatras of the zodiac) but he favoured Rohini (bright star Aldebaran which comes closest to the ecliptic of the moon). Daksha cursed Soma to disappear, for his partiality. Soma (moon) prayed to Shiva to escape the curse of his father in law. Shiva reduced the curse to cyclic waxing & waning. The place where he prayed was called Somnath (The Lord of the Moon). The Puranic story is an allegory encoding natural phenomena of the moon as well as Rohini. The earliest name of Somnath is Prabhasa, mentioned in the Mahabharat as a great tirtha. The Pandavas visited Prabhasa on pilgrimage and Arjuna sought Krishna’s help for the war here. The ancient Somnath temple was vandalized by Mahmud of Ghazni in 1026CE. It went through cycles of rebuilding and destruction over the next ten centuries. The current grand temple was rebuilt after independence under the guidance of Sardar Patel and KM Munshi, both of whom hailed from Gujarat. It was inaugurated by the first President Dr Rajendra Prasad. Source: Stories of Lord Shiva, Rama Krishna Mutt Picture Credit: Gujarat Tourism

hook: Shiva named Kashi, ‘Avimukta’ - which He will never leave. He settled there to reassure someone. Who?
explanation: Quick Read: Shiva found Kashi to satisfy his in-laws who were not happy with his austere tent at Kailasha. Note: Kashi ( The Luminous Place) is where the Shiva first appeared on earth as Jyotirlinga (pillar of light). It was the place first created by Shiva & Shakti. From Kashi they created the rest of the universe. In a lesser known story, Devi Parvati’s parents, Mena & Himalaya were not happy with the tent of Shiva at Kailasha. They desired a proper place for her daughter. So Shiva scanned the whole earth and then came to Kashi. He vowed never to leave it. Vishwanath temple represents the Jyotirlinga but the whole of Kashi is the abode of Shiva – “Kashi ka kankar Shiva Shankar” (the very stones of Kashi are Shiva). Source: Rana B Singh , Dolls of India Picture Credit: Ganga at Kashi, Ramanujam S. R

hook: Gauri Kund is the last motorable stop before the Kedarnath trek starts. How is Gaurikund important in Shiva’s life?
explanation: Quick read: Parvati did Tapas at Gauri Kund seeking Shiva’s hand in marriage Parvati, Himalya’s daughter wanted to marry Shiva and decided to do tapas to win Him over. Convinced by her austerities Shiva appeared in disguise. He asked Parvati why she wanted to marry a mendicant like him, with no lineage and clan. Parvati said that Shiva was unchanging & indescribable but assumed forms for the welfare of bhaktas; and that she would only marry Him. Satisfied, Shiva revealed Himself and asked for Her hand. A small temple at Gauri Kund marks the place of Ma Parvati’s tapas. The marriage took place at nearby Triyuginarayan. Pilgrims start walking from Gaurikund to Kedarnath, the Lord of Cedar trees, and the northernmost JyotirLinga. It is said that the Pandavas wanted to worship Shiva at Kashi to expiate their sin of waging a great war. Shiva refused to meet them and left for the Himalayas. He hid as a bull inside the earth. The Pandavas recognized parts of the bull’s body jutting out and asked for forgiveness. The five places where the bull jutted out became the Panch Kedaras – Kedarnath being the hump of the bull. Source: “India, A Sacred Geography”, Diana Eck Picture Credit: Gauri Mata temple at Gauri Kund, Ramanujam SR

hook: Which Jyotirlinga is said to be at the navel or the centre of the universe?
explanation: Quick read: The Mahakala at Ujjain is the Lord of time and death; and Ujjain is profusely referred in history and puranas. Ujjain is as old as history. It is attested from Buddha’s time. It was the capital of Avanti, one of the 16 mahajanapadas. In the epics, it is called the city victorious i.e. Avantika or Ujjaini. It flourished under the Guptas in the 4th-6th cent CE. It was Kalidasa’s hometown. Ancient and modern Literature speak of Ujjain as the centre of India. It is the navel, the nabhi. Puranas state that Ujjain is not only the centre of the universe but is on the vertical axis of the three worlds. Mahakala at Ujjain is the Lord of time and death. Shiva was bathed first thing in the morning with ash from a funeral pyre. Nowadays ash from burnt cow dung is used. Mahakal is also called Dakshinamoorti, one that faces the South. Ujjain is one of the most sacred tantra temples. The temple has five levels including an underground one. There are three Lingas, the Omkareshwar is on the level above the Mahakal and the Nagchandreshwar, open only on Nag Panchami, on the third level. One unique feature is that the prasad offered here to the Lord can be reoffered. Source: Dwadasa Jyotirlinga, Kanchi Kamakoti Peetha Picture Credit: Wikimedia Commons

hook: Which great river originates near the Trymbakeshwara Jyotirlinga near Nashik?
explanation: Quick read: Godavari tumbled down Brahmagiri towards Tryambakeshwar to cleanse Rishi Gautam._x000D_ _x000D_ Trymbaka means the ‘The three eyed one.” The Linga is ‘Three Eyed’ as it represents the trinity of Brahma, Vishnu & Mahesh. While the site is ancient, the current temple built in black stone and the north Indian nagara style was built in the 18th cent by Peshwa Balaji Baji Rao._x000D_ _x000D_ Godavari originates at Brahmagiri near Tryambakeshwar. Rishi Gautam, whose ashram was in this area, once killed a cow by accident. To cleanse himself, he prayed to Shiva to send the river Ganga to him. Soon Ganga flowed down to him, taking the name Godavri. Seeing the miracle, the Gods beseeched Shiva to make Trymbaka his permanent abode._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Stories of Lord Shiva, Rama Krishna Mutt_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: The source of Godavari, Ramanujam S. R

hook: Shiva slayed rakshasa Bhima at Bhimashankar in Maharashtra. Which Ramayana character was the father of Bhima?
explanation: Quick read: Bhima was Kumbhakarna’s Son and wanted to take revenge on Vishnu (Rama). Bhimashankar Jyotirlinga is located atop a hill between Pune and Nashik. Bhima, a rakshasa, stayed in the region with his single mother Karkati. Bhima came to know that his father was Kumbhakarna and that he was killed by Rama before he could take Karkati back to Lanka. Bhima wanted to take revenge on Vishnu. He prayed to Brahma who gave him boons. Bhima started creating havoc. Devas prayed to Shiva to stop him. Another story suggests that Shiva took the Rudra avatar to kill the powerful Tripurasura who was trying to destroy the three worlds. In a long and furious battle, Shiva destroyed the demon and got the name Tripurantaka. After battle, he sat down from exhaustion and the sweat from his body started flowing as the Bhima river, which originates at Bhimashankar and merges with Krishna. Assam also claims this Jyotirlinga. Source: Shiv Purana, B.K. Chaturvedi Picture Credit: Team BHP

hook: Which Jyotirlinga was inadvertently established by Ravana?
explanation: Quick read: Ravana had to leave the Linga he was carrying from Kailasha at Vaidyanath There are many claimants to the Vaidyanatha Jyotirlinga but the one in Jharkhand is the most well known. Ravana had gone to Kailasha and performed various austerities seeking a Linga for Lanka. Shiva was moved by his discipline and gave him a Linga, with a condition that he could not put it down until he got back to Lanka. The Devas were uneasy seeing the powerful Linga with him. Varuna, the God of Water entered Ravana’s body so that he could not fight the urge to relieve himself. He gave the Linga to a cowherd (or Ganesha in some stories) who could not mange the heavy weight, as Ravana was taking too long to relieve himself. He put the Linga down. When Ravana returned, he could not move it. In ardent devotion, Ravana cut off nine of his ten heads and offered to Shiva. Ravana’s head were restored by Shiva but the Linga did not move and took residence there as Vaidyanatha – the Lord of Physicians. Also known as Babadham, the temple is known for the annual pilgrimage when devotees carry water from the Ganga, eighty miles away, and bring it to the Linga as offering. The pilgrims chant ‘bol bam’ all the way. Source: “India, A Sacred Geography”, Diana Eck Picture Credit: William Hodges painting of Vaidyanath, 1782. British Library

hook: Rameshwaram has two Lingas. One was brought from the Himalayas by Hanuman. Who created the other?
explanation: Quick read: Sita created a Linga since Hanuman was late in bringing a Linga from Kailasha._x000D_ _x000D_ Rama sent Hanuman to the Himalayas to bring an appropriate stone from Kailasha. But Hanuman did not return in time for consecration. So Sita ma created a Linga out of sand which Rama consecrated. Hanuman returned and wanted to replace the sand Linga with the Linga he had brought. However, Hanumanji could not move Sita ma’s linga no matter how hard he tried. Thus there are two Lingas – the Himalayan stone called Vishwanatha and the sand linga called Ramnatha._x000D_ _x000D_ There is also a belief that Rama consecrated the Linga before the war and not after the war._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Dwadasa Jyotirlinga, Kanchi Kamakoti Peetha_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Rameswaram temple, Ramanujam S. R

hook: Srisailam is a Jyotirlinga which is also a Shakti Peeth. Where is Srisailam?
explanation: Quick read: Srisailam is one of the two Jyotirlingas in South India and also one of the few ShaktiPeethas in South India. Mallikarjuna is at Sri Saila (sacred mountain) in Andhra, a site mentioned in the Mahabharata. Being remote, the ancient temple has retained its serene beauty and features. The outer wall or prakara is one of the largest and most detailed in India with bas reliefs going back to 12th cent CE. Srisailam is the Jyotirlinga of Lord Mallikarjuna and the Shakti Peeth of Goddess Bharamaramba. Parvati took the form of a thousand bees (Bharamaramba ) to kill the demon Arunasura. This Jyotirlinga is revered by the local tribals also. Lord Shiva came to Srisailam on a hunting expedition and fell in love with a beautiful Chenchu tribal woman whom he married. She used to accompany him in his expeditions. Shiva is called as Chenchu Mallaiah by the tribes. In a bas relief in the temple, Shiva is shown accompanied by Parvati as a forest woman with arrows and four dogs. Srisailam is also the place where Shiva & Parvati settled to be close to Lord Karthikeya who left Kailash after being upset after losing a contest with his brother Ganesha. He had settled in the south at Palani in Tamil Nadu. This story explains a great Indian mystery. Somewhere in the Gupta period, the worship of Skanda stopped in North India. But He remains very popular in the south worshipped as Skanda, Murugan, Kartikeya, Kumara or Kumaraswami. Source: “Temples and Legends of Andhra Pradesh,” Bharatiya Vidya Bhavan. Picture Credit: Srisailam, Srisailam Devasthanam

hook: Omkareshwar is on the banks of which great river of India?
explanation: Quick read: Omkareshwara is on an island which is in the shape of Om and is formed by the river Narmada. Omkareshwara is at Mandhata in Madhya Pradesh set in the Vindhya mountains. Vindhya felt humiliated when Narada told him that Mount Meru was taller. Vindhya established the Omkareshwara and prayed to Shiva who granted him a boon that he would be the tallest mountain. In arrogance, Vindhya started growing and would not stop even when he blocked the sun’s course. The earth started getting scorched and Shiva sent Agastya to straighten the Vindhya. When Agastya came, Vindhya bowed and asked what he could do. Agastya asked him to remain bowed till he returned. The sage proceeded south where he settled down and never returned. Vindhya stayed with the head bowed and didn’t grow henceforth. Shiva is the Cosmic Sound Aum here – the three primary sound ‘a’, ‘u’ and ‘m’ said to correspond with Brahma, Vishnu & Mahesh. Omkareshwara is an island almost shaped like the Aum in the river Narmada and is a key spot for the iconic Narmada yatra. Source: Dwadasa Jyotirlinga, Kanchi Kamakoti Peetha Picture Credit: Ramanujam S. R

hook: Ghushmeshwara in Maharashtra is located near a famous UNESCO site. Which is the site?
explanation: Quick read: The Joytirlinga marks the devotion of Ghushma who continued her puja even after knowing her son has been killed. Ghushmeshwara or Grishneshwara is located around a km from Ellora caves. The official depiction of the Linga shows it with light radiating at the base, with a man, Sudharma kneeling before it. His mother Ghushma was an ardent Shiv Bhakt and had a daily routine of making 101 parthiv (earth) lingas which she would then immerse in a tank. Sudharma’s step mother killed him out of jealousy, cut the body into pieces and threw it into the same pond where Ghushma would immerse the Shiv Lingas. Even after knowing about her Son’s death, Ghushma carried on with her daily routine. When she went to offer the lingas in a pond as usual, she saw her son alive at the pond due to the grace of Shiva. In recognition of her ardent devotion, Shiva stayed back as Ghushmeshwara, Ghushma’s Lord. Source: Shiv Puran, B.K Chaturvedi Picture Credit: The Kailasanatha temple at Ellora, Lonely Planet

hook: The Nageshwarnath Jyotirlinga is near one of the original Char Dhams. Where is it?
explanation: Quick read: The Jyotirlinga is near Dwarka which is one of the original char dhams._x000D_ _x000D_ Nageshwarnath is located in Darukavana near Dwaraka. There are other claimants for this Jyotirlinga in Pune and Uttarakhand. It refers to a forest of deodar tress. A Shiv Bhakt Supriya was kidnapped by the demon Daruka and his wife Daruki. Supriya created a linga and started worshipping it in the prison. An impressed Shiva gifted Pashupati Astra to Supriya with which he started killing the demons. The place where Supriya worshipped Shiva is near Dwaraka._x000D_ _x000D_ Legend has it that a devotee Namdev was blocking the view of other devotees who asked him to move. Namdev asked them to take him to a place where the Lord does not exist. The other devotees carried Namdev south. When they returned, they found that the Linga itself had turned and was facing south._x000D_ _x000D_ This ancient tirtha and its surroundings have intimate connections to the events in Krishna’s life including where He left his earthly body. Dwarka is part of the char dham circuit – it is believed that every day, Lord Vishnu takes his bath at Rameshwaram (south), meditates at Badrinath (north), dines at Puri (east) and retires at Dwarka (west)._x000D_ Adi Shankara also established mathas at the four corners of the country – Sringeri (south), Jyotirmath (north), Puri (east) and Dwarka (west)._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Art of Living_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture Credit: Gujarat Tourism

hook: India’s oldest university produced Chanakya and Panini. Which is it?
explanation: Quick Read: Taxila is believed to be the oldest university of India and was in existence during Buddha’s time. Taxila or Takshashila, is in modern-day Pakistan. It is Bharat’s oldest centre of learning. It was the capital of Gandhara. It is believed to have been founded by Bharata (Sri Rama’s brother) and named after his son Taksha. It was destroyed by white Huns in 5th century CE. It attracted students from all over India (as far as Benares from the east) for higher education and specialization. It was famous for its philosophers in the days of Alexander. Panini (who wrote Ashtadayi on Sanskrit grammar in 6th cent BCE) and Chanakya (the author of the Arthashastra on statecraft in 4th cent BCE) are from Taxila. King Prasenajit of Kosala, a contemporary of the Buddha, was educated there. So was Prince Jivaka, an illegitimate son of King Bimbisara of Magadha. He learnt medicine & surgery and became a renowned practitioner. Taxila taught Vedas, arts, medicine, law, military and many other technical fields. 103 princes from different parts of India were enrolled in its archery school. On graduation, students also undertook foreign visits to learn further. Source: Dr P Suresh, Higher Educational Institutions in Ancient Indian History Radhakumud Mukherjee, Ancient Indian Education. Picture: Dharmarajika stupa at Taxila. Sasha Isachenko, Wikimedia Commons

hook: This famous university of India had entrance tests for admission and coaching centres nearby. Which is this?
explanation: Quick read: Nalanda had entrance tests conducted at its gates, and admitted only 20% of aspirants. Nalanda was already well known during the time of Mahavira and Buddha. Kundalpur, one of three claimed birthplaces of Mahavira, is near Nalanda. Buddha meditated and taught at the nearby Rajgir. Nalanda was known as a centre of learning during Magadha period. Its growth as a university started during the Gupta period. It became the most sought after university across Asia and focused mainly on higher studies. It received endowments from several Kings which helped students focus on studies. Revenue of 100 villages were allocated to Nalanda to support the education system. Students came from China, Tibet, Korea and Mongolia to study in Nalanda. Scholars guarded the entrance gates of Nalanda and conducted oral tests for aspirants. Only 20% of the aspirants could successfully clear these tests and gain admission. It boasted a splendid library called Dharmaranjana spread over three buildings, one of which was nine storeys high. It was destroyed by Bakhtyar Khilji in late 12th cent CE, and it is said to have burnt for months. Chinese scholars made copies of sacred Buddhist texts from Nalanda. I-tsing copied 400 Sanskrit works amounting to 5,00,000 verses. Any association with Nalanda evoked respect across Asia. We can unequivocally state that never again has a university as renowned as Nalanda existed in India. Sahana Singh writes that a system of coaching existed nearby to help aspirants (Byju’s would have liked to acquire Nalanda!) Source: Dr P Suresh, Higher Educational Institutions in Ancient Indian History Sahana Singh, Educational Heritage of Ancient India Picture: Nalanda University, S. R Ramanujam

hook: Which dynasty of Bengal patronized a string of famous universities like Vikramshila and Somapura, between 8th-12th cent CE?
explanation: Quick Read: The Palas of Bengal and Magadha maintained a network of five universities in the region. Pala period saw the growth of a number of monasteries in ancient Bengal and Magadha. Tibetan sources point out five great Mahaviharas: Vikramasila, Nalanda, Somapura, Odantapura, and Jaggadala. These formed a network and teachers moved from one to another. All of them were under state supervision and the universities co-ordinated with each other. All these universities fell under the invasions of the Delhi sultans. Source: Dr P Suresh, Higher Educational Institutions in Ancient Indian History Picture: Ruins of Vikramsila. Surav Sen, Wikimedia Commons

hook: This ancient centre of learning attracted wise men across religions. Which is it?
explanation: Kashi was a centre of learning cutting across Hindu, Buddhist Jain and Sikh religions. Parsvanath, the 23rd Jain Tirthankara and three other Thirthankaras were born in Kashi. Buddha’s first sermon was at Sarnath near Benares. He taught the essentials of Buddhism to his first five disciples. Kashi was a great centre of Upanishadic learning. Shankaracharya and Ramanuja lived and composed their works in Kashi. Guru Nanak, Guru Teg Bahadur and Guru Gobind Singh visited Kashi. Sant Ramanand, Sant Kabir, Sant Ravidas and Sant Surdas belonged to Kashi. Kashi like now, was a magnet for wise men even then. The earliest Buddhist texts call Sarnath ‘Isipatana’ – a place where holy men landed. True to its name, it remains a centre of pilgrimage for Buddhists, Jains & Hindus. Source: https://www.peepultree.world/livehistoryindia/story/monuments/sarnath GS Randhawa, IIT Roorkee Picture: Wikimedia Commons

hook: The Gargi college and Maitryi Colleges in Delhi are named after Hindu women seers and debaters. Where can you read about them?
explanation: Quick Read: Brhadaranyaka Upanishad describes the famous debates of Gargi and Maitryi with Yajnavalkya _x000D_ _x000D_ Gargi was a philosopher who lived during the time of King Janaka, according to the Brhadaranyaka Upanishad. She was bestowed with the title of BrahmaVadini. She participated in the philosophic debate organized by King Janaka._x000D_ _x000D_ Yajnavalkya considered himself invincible in debates. Gargi started asking questions that Yajnavalkya could not answer. Gargi continued day in and out to test and challenge Yajnavalkya on the soul, existence, the universe and the environment. The questions drove him to a point where he beseeched Gargi to stop._x000D_ _x000D_ Maitryi is credited with 10 hymns out of 1000+ verses in the Rig Veda. Maitryi was the niece of Gargi and was the second wife of Yajnavalkya. He wanted to take sanyas and wished to divide his material wealth between his two wives. Maitryi then debated him on the meaning of wealth. This debate is captured in one of the most important sections of the Brhadaranyaka Upanishad._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Radhakumud Mukherjee, Ancient Indian Education_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture: Gargi College

hook: What did the ‘samavartana’ ceremony and ‘snataka dharma’ mean in ancient education?
explanation: Quick Read: Samavartana is the graduation ceremony which included reciting of the Snataka Dharma or vow. Vidyarambham (in south) or Haathekhori (in Bengal) marked a baby writing the alphabet. ‘Upanayana’ or the thread ceremony symbolized initiation into studies. At the end of a student’s studies, the student went through the ‘samavartana’ or the graduation ceremony. Graduation was an important rite of passage in life. In the presence of students, teachers and invited guests, the graduating student offered guru Dakshina (gift to guru). Thereafter the guru recited the snataka-dharma from the Taittirya Upanishad. This was followed by homa (fire ritual) and snana (ceremonial bath). The Snataka Dharma recites vows that include, “ never deviate from truth, never deviate from dharma, never neglect your well being, never neglect your health, never neglect worldly activities, never neglect self study and teaching of Vedas”. The Taittiriya Upanishad sloka, “…. Acharya Devo Bhava, Athithi Devo Bhava,” was also recited at this time. Even today, a graduate with a bachelor’s degree is called a ‘snataka’ in Hindi. Some graduation ceremonies are even now called Samavartana. Source: Sahana Singh, Educational Heritage in Ancient India Picture: RK Mission invitation for Samavartana function

hook: Which Hindi title for vice chancellor of university came from Gurukul - same as the person in a gurukul in charge of the intellectual and physical health of the students.
explanation: Quick Read: Kulpati was the chief administrator of a gurukul and the title of the Vice Chancellor flows from this role. मुनीनां दशसाहस्रं योऽन्नदानादि पोषाणात। अध्यायपति विप्रर्षिरसौ कुलपति: स्मृत:। Sages who nourished ten thousand sages or students and gave education were called the Kulpati. The responsibility for a gurukul student’s mental and intellectual development rested with the Kulpati. Nowadays the term is used for the ‘Vice Chancellor’ of the university. There was even a controversy in Bangalore University on whether the term Kulpati should refer to the Chancellor (Governor of a State) or the Vice Chancellor. Karnataka even amended its Universities Act in 1986 to clarify that Vice-Chancellor will be referred to as Kulpati. Source: https://vediconcepts.org/history-of-gurukul-education-system The Hindu Picture: Singpost for the residence of Kulpati at Bangalore University

hook: Which Indian leader accused British of cutting down the beautiful tree of education and making India more illiterate?
explanation: Quick Read: Mahatma Gandhi made these comments in London round table conference and his follower Shri Dharampal wrote a book on this. Gandhi made the comment at the round table conference at London in 1931. He was challenged by Philip Hartog, British scientist to prove his statement. Later one of Gandhi’s followers, Shri Dharampal, published a book "The Beautiful Tree" on this subject. Using Britishers’ own survey data Dharampal proved how widespread traditional Indian education was in each village before British intervention. He demonstrated the prevalence of girl education, presence of all castes in student & teacher population and vernacular education. He highlighted how the formal system of education introduced by the British killed the village schools. They were deprived of the traditional financing from local communities. Illiteracy grew since the new formal schools were concentrated in towns and not in villages. Source: B Nivedita, “the Destruction of Indian System of Education,” Speech at IIT Madras in 1998 Picture: The Beautiful Tree Book by Shri Dharampal

hook: Vallabhi University, like modern IIMs, specialized in finance and administration. Interestingly, Vallabhi is in the same State as a leading IIM. Where was Vallabhi?
explanation: Quick Read: Vallabhi is not far from IIM Ahmedabad. _x000D_ _x000D_ Vallabhi taught Niti (Political Science, Statesmanship), Varta (Business, Agriculture), Administration, Theology, Law, Economics and Accountancy. Students from Vallabhi used to present themselves at the Courts of Kings to demonstrate their administrative talent to be employed in Government services. The Kathasaritsagara tells a story of how a Brahmin of the Ganga valley chose to send his son to Vallabhi and not Nalanda as he wanted him to serve in a royal court. It is fitting that IIM Ahmedabad is close to this ancient university._x000D_ _x000D_ Valabhi (modern Vala in Kathiawad) was termed by Hiuen Tsang on par with Nalanda. It flourished about the same time as Nalanda in the 4 to 7th centuries CE. Vallabhi has been a famous Jain center. In 5th cent CE the Vallabhi council of Jains wrote down their religious canon. It was the capital of the Maithraka kingdom and was destroyed in 775CE, likely by the early Muslim invasions but historians are not sure._x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Dr P Suresh, Higher Educational Institutions in Ancient Indian History_x000D_ Radhakumud Mukherjee, Ancient Indian Education_x000D_ D.G. Apte, Universities in Ancient India_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture: IIM Ahmedabad Campus

hook: Which university was famous for logic and jurisprudence and connected to Ramayana?
explanation: Quick Read: Mithila specialized in Nyaya Shastra. It forbid taking notes and learning had to be memorized. _x000D_ _x000D_ Mithila (claimed by both Bihar & Nepal) was the seat of King Janaka who figures prominently as an intellectual patron in the Upanishads and is well known as Sita’s father. It is hard to say whether these refer to the same person or Janaka was a royal title of the region._x000D_ _x000D_ A tradition of philosophical debate emerged in the region and carried on till the 20th cent CE. It was not concentrated in large buildings and it was spread out in Gurukuls. Therefore it escaped the fate of Nalanda and others. It was famous for Nyaya Shastra (logic). So esteemed was the university that Akbar made some grants._x000D_ _x000D_ Gangesa Upadhyaya lived in Mithila in the 12th Century and wrote the Tattva Chintamani propounding Navya Nyaya (New Logic). Subhash Kak has pointed out that George Boole, one of the pioneers of computers, was influenced by Navya Nyaya as attested by letters written by his wife, Mary Boole._x000D_ _x000D_ One intriguing aspect of Mithila university was that instruction was entirely oral, students were allowed to memorise but not take down notes. An angry student, Sarvabhuma, memorized the teachings and set up his own university at Nadia that eclipsed Mithila in Nyaya shastra. Notes were allowed at Nadia!_x000D_ _x000D_ Source: Sahana Singh, Educational Heritage of Ancient India_x000D_ _x000D_ Radhakumud Mukherjee, Ancient Indian Education_x000D_ _x000D_ Picture: Yajnavalkya and King Janaka

hook: In South India, kings patronized self governing settlements of Brahmins who then provided free education to people. What are they called?
explanation: Quick Read: The kings and the society supported Agraharam as a settlement of Brahmins which provided free education to people. In south India, agraharas served as centers of learning and teaching. An agrahara was a whole settlement of learned Brahmins. It was typically, but not always, two rows of houses along a street with a temple at one end. It had its own powers of government and was maintained by generous donations from the society. South Indian kingdoms also had other cultural institutions known as Ghatika and Brahmapuri. A Ghatika was a centre of learning including religion and was small in size. Temples, Mathas, Jain Basadis and Buddhist Viharas also existed as other sources of learning during this period. An inscription at Ennayiram in Tamil Nadu describes in great detail how temple schools and universities were financed by the Cholas. Trivandrum housed the Kanthalloor Sala, known as the Nalanda of South India and taught around 65 subjects. Source: NCERT, Ancient Education System of India Picture: A Jain Agraharam in Mannargudi, Tamil Nadu. S. R Ramanujam

hook: Many Indian kings were staunch supporters of education. Which famous Bangalore institution sits on a 317 acre prime land gifted by Mysore Maharajah?
explanation: Quick Read: The Mysore Maharajah supported JN Tata’s dream of setting up IISc with land and endowments. The Indian Institute of Science was started in 1909 in Bangalore. It was the brainchild of JN Tata (IISc is still called Tata Institute locally; he would die before it started though). The Mysore Kingdom provided the financial muscle. It contributed 371 acres of land in Bangalore, funds for capital expenditure and an annual grant. Later on, C.V Raman, the Nobel Laureate would become the first Indian Director of IISc and start the department of Physics. In the 19th and 20th century, the Mysore Kingdom made major contributions to education under Krishnaraja III, Chamaraja X and Krishnaraja IV. The first free school for English education was established in 1833 in Mysore. Later on the Kingdom would establish special boarding schools for backward classes, college for girls, introduce reservation and mid-day meals. Mysore University and several technical institutions were established by the kingdom. Source: IISC Karnataka Gazetteer, Mysore District, 1988 M.R.
